planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring with a Japanese plum pollinator.

directions: Set the tree in full sun and free-draining soil, and pair it with another Japanese plum for fruit set. Known for its deep red, firm flesh, it holds well after picking. Harvest the dark red fruit in late summer when it softens slightly and the juicy interior sweetens.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring with a Japanese plum pollinator.

directions: Plant this large-fruited Japanese plum in full sun and well-drained ground in a mild-winter area. It needs a compatible Japanese plum nearby to pollinate. Harvest the big, yellow-to-red fruit with fragrant, amber flesh in midsummer once it softens and grows aromatic.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; needs a long, warm season.

directions: Set this heat-loving Japanese plum in full sun where the season is long enough to ripen it. Grow a compatible Japanese plum nearby for cross-pollination. Pick the large, heart-shaped, greenish-yellow fruit in late summer to early autumn when the firm flesh softens and sweetens.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring with a Japanese plum pollinator.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, and provide a compatible Japanese plum for pollination. This late variety produces large, firm fruit that ships and stores well. Harvest the big, black-skinned plums with amber flesh in late summer once they soften and sweeten.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; cold-hardy hybrid.

directions: Plant this cold-hardy American-Japanese hybrid in full sun and well-drained soil. It is a strong pollinator for other Japanese-type plums and crops well itself with a partner. Harvest the reddish-bronze, spicy-sweet fruit in late summer once it softens and the aroma develops.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; hardy hybrid needing a pollinator.

directions: Set this hardy American-Japanese hybrid in full sun and well-drained soil, and grow a compatible pollinator such as Toka nearby. It bears large fruit at a young age and needs thinning to avoid overloading. Harvest the big, red, yellow-fleshed plums in late summer when they soften and sweeten.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; very cold-hardy hybrid.

directions: Plant this very cold-hardy hybrid plum in full sun and well-drained soil, with a compatible pollinator like Toka nearby. It is productive on the northern plains where tender plums fail. Harvest the large, red, sweet fruit in late summer once it softens and colors fully.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; bred for the humid Southeast.

directions: Plant this disease-tolerant Japanese plum, bred for the humid Southeast, in full sun and well-drained soil. Grow a compatible Japanese plum nearby to ensure a crop. Harvest the red-purple, sweet fruit in early to midsummer when it softens on the tree.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; weeping ornamental-edible form.

directions: Plant this weeping, self-fertile form of Santa Rosa in full sun where its cascading branches can be admired. It doubles as an ornamental and a fruit tree in smaller gardens. Harvest the reddish-purple, amber-fleshed plums in midsummer when they soften and sweeten.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; the classic drying prune.

directions: Plant this classic French drying prune in full sun and deep, well-drained soil. It is self-fertile and prized for its very high sugar content, ideal for drying. Harvest the small, dark reddish-purple fruit in early autumn when fully soft and richly sweet, then dry for prunes.

planting.note: Set out dormant bare-root or potted trees in early spring as soon as the ground can be worked.

directions: Plant a grafted tree with the graft union a few inches above the soil surface and stake it through the first season. Bing needs a compatible pollinizer nearby such as Van or Rainier, so plant at least two different sweet cherries. Prune to an open center for good light, and pick the large dark-mahogany fruit in early summer once it colors fully and tastes rich and sweet.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring in deep, well-drained soil.

directions: Give this yellow-blushed cherry full sun and a second sweet cherry as a pollinizer since it will not set fruit alone. Keep the trunk staked early and thin crossing branches in late winter for airflow. Netting helps because birds favor these bright fruits; harvest when the skin turns golden with a rosy blush and the flesh is firm and honey-sweet.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a single tree will fruit on its own.

directions: Stella is self-fertile, so one tree can bear a crop and it also pollinizes many other sweet cherries. Set the graft union above the soil line and water deeply during dry spells while fruit swells. Prune lightly after harvest rather than in wet weather to reduce disease; pick the dark-red fruit in early to midsummer once it is fully colored and sweet.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in early spring while still dormant.

directions: This self-fertile cultivar produces large, firm, dark cherries without a partner tree. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage and stake young trees against wind. Its firm fruit resists cracking better than many, but avoid overhead watering near ripening; harvest the glossy near-black cherries in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in a sunny, sheltered spot.

directions: Sweetheart is self-fertile and one of the latest sweet cherries to ripen, extending the season. Set out a grafted tree with the union above the soil and train to an open framework. Because it ripens over a stretched period you can pick repeatedly; gather the heart-shaped bright-red fruit in late summer as each batch sweetens.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring alongside another sweet cherry.

directions: Van is a reliable, heavy-bearing pollinizer for Bing and other sweet cherries but needs a partner itself. Plant in full sun, stake early, and thin the abundant crop lightly so fruit sizes up. Harvest the firm dark-red cherries in early summer when they are fully colored and richly flavored.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in deep, well-drained ground.

directions: This vigorous old variety ripens early and serves as a good pollen source for other sweet cherries, though it needs a pollinizer too. Give it room, full sun, and yearly dormant pruning to keep the vigorous canopy open. Pick the small-to-medium purplish-black fruit in early summer when soft and intensely sweet, as it does not ship well and is best eaten fresh.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring with a compatible pollinizer nearby.

directions: Kordia, also sold as Attika, bears late-season dark heart-shaped cherries prized for firmness and flavor. It is not self-fertile, so pair it with Regina, Van, or another compatible cultivar. Prune in dry summer weather to limit canker; harvest the deep mahogany fruit in mid to late summer once fully dark and firm.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; provide a suitable pollen partner.

directions: Regina is a late, crack-resistant German cultivar with firm dark fruit that holds well on the tree. It requires cross-pollination, so plant Kordia or another compatible variety close by. Keep young trees staked and pruned to an open center; harvest the large glossy cherries in late summer when uniformly dark and firm.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a single tree will crop.

directions: Sunburst is self-fertile and yields large, soft, dark-red cherries good for fresh eating. Plant in full sun with good drainage and stake the young tree. Because the fruit is tender, pick promptly and handle gently; harvest in midsummer when the cherries are big, dark, and sweet.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in a sunny, well-drained site.

directions: Skeena is a self-fertile late-season cherry producing large, firm, dark fruit with good crack resistance. Set the graft above the soil, stake early, and prune in dry weather to limit disease. Harvest the heavy crop of near-black cherries in late summer when firm and fully colored.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring with a compatible pollinizer.

directions: Royal Ann, essentially the same as Napoleon, is a classic yellow cherry with a red blush long used for brining and canning. It needs cross-pollination from another sweet cherry that flowers at the same time. Guard against birds and rain-cracking near harvest; pick in early to midsummer when the fruit is amber-gold blushed with pink.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring beside a pollen partner.

directions: Utah Giant produces very large, firm, dark cherries with excellent sweet flavor and needs a pollinizer such as Van or Bing. Plant in full sun, stake early, and prune to keep the canopy open and productive. Harvest in early to midsummer when the big fruit is dark red and firm to the touch.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring with a compatible pollinizer nearby.

directions: This dependable late German cultivar bears firm, dark, crack-resistant cherries and pollinizes well with many varieties. It needs cross-pollination, so plant a second compatible tree. Train to an open center and prune in dry weather; harvest the glossy dark fruit in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring with a suitable pollen partner.

directions: Emperor Francis is a firm yellow cherry with a red blush, similar to Napoleon and used fresh and for processing. It requires cross-pollination from another sweet cherry blooming at the same time. Protect ripening fruit from rain to limit cracking; harvest in early to midsummer when amber-yellow washed with red.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; provide a compatible pollinizer.

directions: Nicknamed the black cherry for its very early dark fruit, Chelan opens the sweet cherry season and needs a pollen partner. Give it full sun and open-center pruning for airflow. Harvest the medium-large mahogany cherries in late spring to early summer, ahead of most other varieties.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring in a sunny, drained site.

directions: Benton is a self-fertile mid-season cherry with large, firm, dark-red fruit and productive habit. Plant it alone or as a pollinizer for other cultivars, staking young trees against wind. Prune in dry weather and harvest the firm dark cherries in midsummer when fully colored.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring with a compatible pollinizer.

directions: Sam is a hardy, upright-growing black cherry valued as a pollinizer and for its firm, crack-resistant fruit. It needs cross-pollination from another sweet cherry, so plant a second variety. Train the vigorous, upright branches to an open form; harvest the dark firm cherries in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; largely self-fertile but crops best near others.

directions: This very early Spanish cherry is low-chill and largely self-fertile, making it useful in milder climates. Plant in full sun and stake young trees, keeping the canopy open. Harvest the small-to-medium red cherries in late spring, among the first sweet cherries to ripen.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring with a compatible pollinizer.

directions: Early Robin is a blush-type yellow cherry similar to Rainier but ripening earlier, needing a pollen partner. Give it full sun, stake the young tree, and net against birds that favor pale fruit. Harvest in early summer when the skin is golden with a red blush and the flesh is sweet and firm.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; a single tree crops well.

directions: Montmorency is the classic self-fertile pie cherry, bearing bright-red tart fruit on a compact, hardy tree. Plant in full sun with good drainage and stake the young tree; it fruits earlier in life than sweet cherries. Harvest the clear-red sour cherries in early to midsummer for pies, preserves, and juice.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; self-fertile and shade-tolerant.

directions: Morello is a self-fertile dark-fleshed sour cherry that tolerates a partly shaded or north-facing wall better than most fruit. Plant in reasonably drained soil and train against a wall or as a small open tree. Harvest the deep-red, richly acid cherries in mid to late summer for cooking and liqueurs.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; naturally dwarf and self-fertile.

directions: North Star is a genetically dwarf, self-fertile sour cherry rarely topping ten feet, ideal for small gardens. Plant in full sun and it will fruit young with little pruning beyond removing dead wood. Its compact size makes netting easy; harvest the dark-red tart cherries in midsummer for pies and preserves.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; semi-dwarf and self-fertile.

directions: Meteor is a hardy semi-dwarf sour cherry with bright-red fruit and yellow flesh, self-fertile and very cold-tolerant. Plant in full sun with good drainage; its modest size keeps harvest and netting manageable. Pick the tart red cherries in midsummer for pies, jam, and juice.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; self-fertile.

directions: Balaton is a Hungarian dark-fleshed sour cherry, larger, firmer, and sweeter than Montmorency yet still tart. It is self-fertile and ripens a little later, so it extends the pie-cherry season. Plant in full sun with good drainage; harvest the deep-red, richly flavored fruit in midsummer for fresh use and baking.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; self-fertile and early.

directions: Early Richmond is a self-fertile tart cherry that ripens ahead of Montmorency, opening the pie-cherry season. Plant in full sun with good drainage and stake young trees. Harvest the bright-red, soft, juicy cherries in early summer for cooking, as they are too tart to enjoy fresh.

planting.note: Plant dormant trees in early spring; self-fertile.

directions: Danube, a Hungarian morello-type also sold as Erdi Botermo, bears sweet-tart dark-red fruit good enough to eat fresh as well as cook. It is self-fertile and productive on a compact tree. Plant in full sun with good drainage; harvest the dark juicy cherries in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant dormant shrubs in early spring; self-fertile and very cold-hardy.

directions: Carmine Jewel is a dwarf, self-fertile bush cherry from the Romance series, hardy to very cold winters and grown as a multi-stemmed shrub. Plant in full sun with good drainage and it will fruit within a few years with minimal pruning. Harvest the small dark-red, sweet-tart cherries in mid to late summer for fresh eating, juice, and baking.

planting.note: Plant dormant shrubs in early spring; self-fertile and very hardy.

directions: Romeo is a cold-hardy self-fertile bush cherry in the Romance series, ripening a little later than its siblings with dark, richly flavored fruit. Grow it as a shrub in full sun with well-drained soil and prune only to shape or remove dead wood. Harvest the deep-red, sweet-tart cherries in late summer for eating fresh or processing.

planting.note: Plant dormant shrubs in early spring; self-fertile and very hardy.

directions: Juliet is one of the sweeter Romance-series bush cherries, self-fertile and extremely cold-hardy on a compact multi-stemmed shrub. Plant in full sun with good drainage and expect fruit within a few years. Harvest the dark-red, sweet-tart cherries in mid to late summer for fresh eating and preserves.

planting.note: Plant dormant shrubs in early spring; self-fertile and very hardy.

directions: Valentine is a tart, bright-red Romance-series bush cherry favored for pies and juice, self-fertile and hardy to harsh winters. Grow as a shrub in full sun with well-drained soil and minimal pruning. Harvest the firm red cherries in mid to late summer once fully colored and richly tart.

planting.note: Plant dormant shrubs in early spring; self-fertile and hardy.

directions: Crimson Passion is the sweetest of the Romance-series bush cherries, with firm dark fruit and the highest sugar of the group. It is self-fertile but somewhat slower and less vigorous, so give it full sun and patient care. Harvest the rich dark-red cherries in late summer, sweet enough to enjoy straight off the bush.

planting.note: Plant dormant shrubs in early spring; self-fertile and hardy.

directions: Cupid bears the largest fruit of the Romance-series bush cherries on a low, spreading, self-fertile shrub that shrugs off cold winters. Plant in full sun with good drainage and remove only dead or crowded stems. Harvest the big dark-red, sweet-tart cherries in late summer, among the last of the series to ripen.

planting.note: Sow cleaned seed outdoors in fall so winter cold stratifies it, or cold-stratify before spring sowing.

directions: The wild species is a large forest tree and the common rootstock for grafted sweet cherries, bearing small dark fruit. Seed needs several months of cold to germinate, so sow outdoors in fall or stratify before spring planting. Seedlings vary widely; harvest the small, mostly sweet cherries in early summer where trees fruit, though most are grown for rootstock or timber.

planting.note: Set out dormant bare-root or potted trees in early spring before growth resumes.

directions: Plant a grafted tree with the graft union kept above the finished soil line and water deeply through the first summer. Thin young fruit to about three inches apart so the remaining apricots size up and the branches are not overloaded. Pick when the skin turns deep orange with a blush and the fruit parts easily from the spur in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in late winter or early spring in a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: Choose a site with good air drainage to reduce late-frost damage to the early blooms. Thin the crop in spring and prune lightly after harvest to keep the canopy open. Harvest when the fruit is soft, richly aromatic, and nearly saturated orange for the best flavor.

planting.note: Set out dormant trees in early spring; this cultivar blooms slightly late and tolerates cold better than most.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained soil and stake young trees against wind their first year. Thin fruit and remove crossing branches during the dormant season to build a strong frame. Pick the firm, tangy fruit when it flushes gold and yields slightly to a gentle squeeze.

planting.note: A cold-hardy northern selection; plant dormant in early spring where late frosts are a risk.

directions: Give this hardy variety a spot with excellent air movement to protect its buds from spring cold. Thin the set fruit and prune yearly to admit light into the center of the tree. Harvest the round, thick-skinned golden fruit in mid to late summer once fully colored and fragrant.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; this Canadian variety resists brown rot and perennial canker.

directions: Set the grafted tree in fertile, well-drained ground and keep the root zone mulched but clear of the trunk. Thin fruit clusters in late spring and prune to an open vase form. Pick the large orange fruit when it softens and pulls freely in early to midsummer.

planting.note: An early-ripening variety; plant dormant in early spring in a frost-sheltered location.

directions: Plant near another apricot for reliable pollination, since fruit set improves with a partner. Thin heavily-set branches so the large fruit can reach full size. Harvest the firm, early-season fruit as it turns deep orange and begins to soften at the tip.

planting.note: Bred for cool, damp regions; plant dormant in early spring and site for good drainage.

directions: This variety sets fruit well in cooler maritime climates where many apricots struggle. Keep the canopy open with dormant pruning to speed drying after rain and limit disease. Pick the elongated golden fruit in late summer when fully colored and sweet.

planting.note: A late-blooming, cold-tolerant variety; plant dormant in early spring in exposed sites.

directions: Its later bloom often escapes the spring frosts that ruin earlier apricots, making it dependable in the mountain West. Thin the crop and prune yearly for size and light. Harvest the medium, sweet fruit when it colors up and softens in midsummer.

planting.note: A compact, late-blooming variety; plant dormant in early spring for small yards.

directions: The naturally smaller, spreading habit suits it to tighter spaces and container culture. Its late flowers help it dodge frost, and the tree shows good resistance to cracking and disease. Pick the firm orange fruit blushed with red as it ripens in late summer.

planting.note: A very hardy Minnesota selection; plant dormant in early spring and pair with Sungold for pollination.

directions: Plant this variety together with a compatible pollinizer such as Sungold, as it will not set fruit alone. Site it where cold air drains away to protect the early bloom. Harvest the sweet golden fruit when it softens and colors fully in midsummer.

planting.note: Very cold-hardy; plant dormant in early spring and pair with Moongold for cross-pollination.

directions: Grow alongside Moongold so the two can pollinate each other and set a full crop. Keep the tree mulched and pruned to an open center for good light. Pick the round, mild golden fruit blushed with orange as it ripens in summer.

planting.note: An extremely cold-hardy species used as rootstock and for edible fruit; plant dormant in early spring.

directions: Seed requires a long cold-moist stratification before it will sprout, so most gardeners plant grafted or seedling trees instead. This tough species withstands harsh winters and dry sites and is often used as a hardy rootstock. Harvest the small, sometimes tart orange fruit in mid to late summer for eating and preserves.

planting.note: A low-chill variety for mild-winter regions; plant dormant in late winter or early spring.

directions: This variety needs only modest winter chill, making it a good choice for warm valleys and coastal gardens. It is self-fruitful and bears young, so thin the early crop to size the fruit. Pick the large, mildly sweet orange fruit as it softens in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: A low-chill, disease-tolerant variety; plant dormant in late winter or early spring.

directions: Plant this self-fruitful, low-chill variety in a sunny spot with well-drained soil. It shows good resistance to brown rot and needs only light annual pruning. Harvest the sweet, aromatic golden fruit blushed with rose in early summer once it yields to gentle pressure.

planting.note: A large-fruited commercial strain; plant dormant in early spring in a sheltered site.

directions: This vigorous self-fruitful tree produces large fruit prized for canning and fresh eating. Thin the set fruit in spring and keep the canopy open to ripen the crop evenly. Pick the big oval fruit when it turns full gold-orange and softens in mid to late summer.

planting.note: An early-blooming, very large-fruited variety; plant dormant in early spring and provide a pollinizer.

directions: Plant with a second apricot variety nearby, as this one needs cross-pollination to set well. Site it where spring frosts are unlikely because it blooms early. Harvest the very large, oval light-orange fruit when it colors and softens in early to midsummer.

planting.note: A plum-apricot interspecific hybrid; plant dormant in early spring with a compatible pollinizer.

directions: Plant a compatible pluot or Japanese plum nearby to ensure pollination and good fruit set. Thin the crop in spring so the remaining fruit sizes up. Pick the reddish-purple, intensely sweet fruit in late summer when it softens and its aroma deepens.

planting.note: The dappled 'dinosaur egg' pluot; plant dormant in early spring with a pollinizer.

directions: Set out with a compatible Japanese plum or pluot for cross-pollination and reliable crops. Thin the fruit and prune to keep the canopy open and productive. Harvest the mottled green-and-red skinned fruit when its pink flesh turns soft and sweet in late summer.

planting.note: An elongated, crisp late pluot; plant dormant in early spring with a pollinizer.

directions: Plant with a compatible pollinizer such as a Japanese plum for a full set. This variety holds firm and crisp on the tree, so it can be picked over a long window. Harvest the green oblong fruit blushed with pink when it turns crunchy-sweet in early fall.

planting.note: An early, richly flavored pluot; plant dormant in early spring with a pollinizer.

directions: Grow with a compatible plum or pluot nearby, as this early variety needs cross-pollination. Thin developing fruit so the crop reaches full size and flavor. Pick the greenish-red skinned fruit with sweet red flesh when it softens in early summer.

planting.note: A sweet green-gold pluot; plant dormant in early spring with a compatible pollinizer.

directions: Plant near a compatible plum or pluot to guarantee pollination and cropping. Thin the set fruit and prune yearly to keep the tree open and manageable. Harvest the greenish-yellow fruit with amber flesh when it turns soft and honey-sweet in midsummer.

planting.note: A balanced sweet-tart pluot; plant dormant in early spring with a pollinizer.

directions: Pair with a compatible plum or pluot for pollination and a dependable crop. Thin the fruit in spring and open the canopy with dormant pruning. Pick the red-skinned, orange-fleshed fruit when it softens and balances sweet and tart in midsummer.

planting.note: A small, very sweet orange-fleshed pluot; plant dormant in early spring with a pollinizer.

directions: Plant with a compatible pollinizer so this small-fruited variety sets a full crop. Thin lightly, since the fruit is naturally small and heavy-bearing. Harvest the red-orange fruit when its juicy amber flesh turns intensely sweet in mid to late summer.

planting.note: A green-skinned, honey-sweet pluot; plant dormant in early spring with a pollinizer.

directions: Grow near a compatible plum or pluot to ensure pollination and cropping. Thin the fruit and keep the canopy open for good light and air. Harvest the green-to-yellow fruit with amber flesh when it turns soft and very sweet in late summer.

planting.note: An apricot-dominant aprium hybrid; plant dormant in late winter or early spring.

directions: This early aprium is partly self-fruitful but crops much better with an apricot pollinizer nearby. It needs only modest winter chill and bears an apricot-like fruit with extra sweetness. Pick the firm, fuzzy amber fruit as it softens and deepens in flavor in early summer.

planting.note: A sweet, juicy aprium; plant dormant in late winter or early spring with an apricot pollinizer.

directions: Plant with an apricot nearby to improve fruit set on this low-chill aprium. Thin the crop in spring so the remaining fruit reaches full size and sweetness. Harvest the pale, juicy fruit when it softens and turns honey-sweet in early to midsummer.

planting.note: An early plum-apricot cross for the Southeast; plant dormant in late winter or early spring.

directions: This early plumcot performs well in warmer, humid regions and crops better with a Japanese plum pollinizer. Thin the heavy set and prune to an open center for airflow and disease control. Pick the dark purple fruit with red flesh when it softens and sweetens in early summer.

planting.note: An early, large-fruited variety; plant dormant in early spring and provide a pollinizer.

directions: Grow with another apricot nearby, since this variety sets best with cross-pollination. Thin the large fruit in spring and prune to keep the canopy open. Harvest the broad, light-orange fruit blushed with red when it softens in early to midsummer.

planting.note: A late-ripening variety that extends the apricot season; plant dormant in early spring.

directions: This self-fruitful, low-chill variety ripens late and stretches the harvest well into the season. Thin the crop and keep the tree pruned to an open, airy frame. Pick the medium orange fruit when it colors fully and softens in late summer to early fall.

planting.note: An early Pacific Northwest variety; plant dormant in early spring and provide a pollinizer.

directions: Plant with another apricot nearby because this early variety needs a pollinizer to crop well. Thin the fruit in spring and stake young trees against wind. Harvest the roundish, red-blushed fruit when it softens and turns sweet in early summer.

planting.note: Set out grafted trees in spring once hard frost has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Plant a grafted tree in well-drained soil with the graft union kept several inches above the surface, and mulch away from the trunk. Water deeply but let the top few inches dry between soakings, and feed a citrus fertilizer in spring and summer. This seedless variety ripens in winter, so pick when the fruit is fully colored and easily twists free.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost danger in a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: Position this pink-fleshed navel in full sun and give it steady moisture through the fruiting season for the best flavor. Keep the base free of grass and weeds, and prune only to remove crossing or damaged wood. The rosy interior develops in cool weather, so harvest in mid to late winter when the rind is bright orange.

planting.note: Transplant in spring once nights stay reliably above freezing.

directions: Plant this late-season navel with good drainage and protect young trees from wind and cold snaps. Feed lightly through the growing season and avoid soggy roots. Fruit hangs well on the tree into spring, so pick as needed from late winter onward when fully colored.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost in a sunny, sheltered site.

directions: This nearly seedless Valencia selection wants full sun and free-draining soil to thrive. Water deeply and feed regularly during active growth, tapering off in cool months. The juicy, easy-peeling fruit ripens in spring, so pick when deeply colored and richly flavored.

planting.note: Transplant in spring; tolerant of hotter, drier interior climates.

directions: Plant this heat-adapted orange in full sun where summers are warm, keeping the root zone evenly moist. Apply citrus fertilizer in split doses across the growing season. The thin-skinned fruit ripens in late winter to spring; harvest when the rind colors up and the flavor turns sweet.

planting.note: Plant in spring; one of the more cold-tolerant sweet oranges.

directions: Set this early-season juice orange in full sun with well-drained soil and steady moisture. Feed with a balanced citrus fertilizer and mulch to conserve water. The nearly seedless fruit ripens early, so harvest from fall into early winter when fully colored.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny location.

directions: This old early-ripening juice orange grows best in full sun with deep, occasional watering. Fertilize in spring and summer and keep the trunk base clear of mulch. Seedy, aromatic fruit ripens early; pick from fall into early winter as color develops.

planting.note: Transplant in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Plant this richly aromatic juice orange in full sun with fast-draining soil and reliable moisture. Provide citrus feed through the growing season and protect from frost when young. The seedy, sweet-tart fruit ripens mid-season; harvest in winter when the rind is fully orange.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, protected site.

directions: Plant this sweet, low-acid blood orange in full sun with well-drained soil and even watering. Feed through spring and summer and mulch to hold moisture. The flesh blushes red where winters are cool; harvest from mid to late winter when fully flavored.

planting.note: Transplant in spring; a late-ripening blood orange.

directions: Set this late-season blood orange in full sun and give it steady moisture with sharp drainage. Feed with citrus fertilizer during the growing months and shelter young trees from cold. Red-streaked, nearly seedless fruit ripens late; harvest in late winter into spring.

planting.note: Transplant in spring in a warm, sunny spot.

directions: Grow this classic zipper-skin tangerine in full sun with fast-draining soil and regular moisture. Feed during the growing season and protect from frost while young. The tart-sweet, seedy fruit traditionally ripens around midwinter; harvest when deeply colored and easily peeled.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, frost-protected location.

directions: This large, sweet Asian mandarin does best in full sun with deep, even watering and rich, well-drained soil. Feed with citrus fertilizer through spring and summer. The puffy, easy-peeling fruit ripens in early winter; harvest as soon as it colors, since it does not hold long on the tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring; compact habit suits containers.

directions: Plant this tiny seedless mandarin in full sun in the ground or a large pot, using free-draining soil. Water consistently and feed lightly but often during growth. The bite-size, super-sweet fruit ripens in early winter; harvest when the little fruits turn fully orange.

planting.note: Transplant in spring; a very late-ripening mandarin.

directions: Set this seedless mandarin in full sun with well-drained soil and steady moisture. Feed through the growing season and expect alternate-bearing years with lighter crops. The sweet, easy-peel fruit ripens very late, so harvest from spring into early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; late-ripening and seedless.

directions: Plant this bumpy-rind, seedless mandarin in full sun with good drainage and regular watering. Apply citrus fertilizer during spring and summer growth flushes. The rich, sweet fruit ripens late and holds well on the tree; harvest from late winter into spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; sets best fruit with cross-pollination.

directions: Grow this juicy early mandarin in full sun with well-drained soil and consistent moisture. Feed through the growing season and plant a compatible pollinizer nearby for heavier crops. The sweet, somewhat seedy fruit ripens early; harvest from late fall into winter.

planting.note: Transplant in spring in a warm, sunny site.

directions: Plant this deep-orange mandarin in full sun with fast-draining soil and even watering. Feed with citrus fertilizer during active growth and protect young trees from frost. The glossy, richly colored fruit ripens in early winter; harvest when fully colored and easy to peel.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a sweet tangor grown as a mandarin.

directions: Set this exceptionally sweet mandarin hybrid in full sun with well-drained soil and steady moisture. Feed through the growing season and thin fruit in heavy years to avoid limb breakage. The honey-sweet, seedy fruit ripens late; harvest from late winter into spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; isolate from other citrus to keep fruit seedless.

directions: Plant this rich, easy-peel mandarin in full sun with good drainage and even watering. Feed during spring and summer, and keep it away from cross-pollinating citrus to stay seedless. The deeply colored fruit ripens in late winter; harvest when fully orange and richly flavored.

planting.note: Transplant in spring; reliably seedless even near other citrus.

directions: Grow this seedless mandarin in full sun with well-drained soil and consistent moisture. Feed with a citrus formula through the growing season. The sweet, easy-peel fruit ripens in late winter and holds well on the tree; harvest from late winter into spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; vigorous and heavy-bearing.

directions: Plant this vigorous, productive mandarin in full sun with deep, well-drained soil and regular watering. Feed generously during spring and summer and thin fruit to reduce alternate bearing. The very juicy, seedy fruit ripens in winter; harvest when fully colored and richly sweet.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with frost protection when young.

directions: Set this glossy, richly flavored mandarin hybrid in full sun with good drainage and even moisture. Feed through the growing season and grow apart from other citrus to keep seed count low. The deep-orange fruit ripens in early winter; harvest when fully colored.

planting.note: Plant in spring; fruits heavier with a pollinizer nearby.

directions: Plant this small, intensely sweet mandarin hybrid in full sun with well-drained soil and steady watering. Feed during spring and summer, and add a compatible pollinizer for the best set. The rich, juicy fruit ripens from late fall into winter; harvest when fully orange.

planting.note: Plant in spring; the most cold-tolerant common satsuma.

directions: Plant this hardy, easy-peel satsuma in full sun with well-drained soil, keeping the graft union above ground. Water deeply and feed a citrus fertilizer in spring and summer, and protect from hard freezes when young. The sweet, seedless fruit ripens in late fall; harvest as soon as it colors since it puffs if left too long.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a slightly earlier-ripening satsuma.

directions: Set this cold-tolerant satsuma in full sun with fast-draining soil and regular moisture. Feed during the growing season and mulch the root zone, keeping it off the trunk. Sweet, nearly seedless fruit ripens in fall; harvest promptly once colored for the best texture.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an early-ripening Japanese satsuma.

directions: Plant this early, cold-hardy satsuma in full sun with well-drained soil and even watering. Feed a citrus formula in spring and summer and shelter young trees from freezes. The sweet, seedless fruit ripens early in fall; harvest as soon as the rind colors.

planting.note: Plant in spring; compact, early, and cold-tolerant.

directions: Grow this compact early satsuma in full sun with good drainage and steady moisture. Feed through spring and summer and protect from hard cold while establishing. The rich, seedless fruit ripens early in fall; harvest soon after it colors for the sweetest flavor.

planting.note: Plant in spring; prized for very early, rich flavor.

directions: Plant this flavorful early satsuma in full sun with well-drained soil and consistent watering. Feed during the growing season and guard young trees against freezes. The intensely sweet, seedless fruit ripens very early in fall; harvest as soon as the rind turns orange.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Gulf Coast favorite satsuma.

directions: Set this cold-hardy satsuma in full sun with fast-draining soil and regular moisture. Feed with a citrus fertilizer in spring and summer and mulch the roots. The sweet, nearly seedless fruit ripens in fall; harvest promptly once colored for the best eating.

planting.note: Plant in spring; sets best with a compatible pollinizer.

directions: Plant this bell-shaped mandarin-grapefruit hybrid in full sun with well-drained soil and deep watering. Feed through the growing season and add a pollinizer such as a mandarin nearby to improve set. The tart-sweet, easy-peel fruit with its distinctive neck ripens in winter; harvest when fully colored.

planting.note: Plant in spring; benefits from a pollinizer for heavy crops.

directions: Grow this juicy, early tangelo in full sun with good drainage and consistent moisture. Feed with citrus fertilizer through spring and summer and plant a pollinizer for the best fruiting. The sweet, mild fruit ripens earlier than Minneola; harvest from late fall into winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a tender tangor needing warmth.

directions: Plant this richly flavored mandarin-orange hybrid in full sun with well-drained soil and steady watering. Feed during the growing season and protect from cold, as it is more frost-sensitive than most oranges. The spicy-sweet, easy-peel fruit ripens in winter; harvest when deeply colored.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost in a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: Site this vigorous, upright tree where its dense, thorny canopy has room to spread, and stake young trees against wind. It tolerates heat and cold slightly better than Eureka, so give it protection only in a hard frost. Harvest the tart yellow fruit mostly in a heavy late-winter flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring; grows well in a large container that can move indoors for winter.

directions: This lemon-mandarin hybrid stays compact and takes to pots, so use a free-draining citrus mix and a container with ample drainage. Pinch back leggy shoots to keep a bushy shape and thin heavy fruit set on young trees. Pick the fragrant, thin-skinned fruit when it turns a deep egg-yolk orange and gives slightly to the touch.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a large pot or warm bed; very cold-sensitive.

directions: Grow this small, thorny lemon-citron hybrid in a big container so it can shelter indoors during cold snaps. Support the branches, as the enormous, bumpy fruit can weigh several pounds each and bend the limbs. Harvest when the thick-rinded fruit turns fully yellow, and expect very seedy but usable flesh.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to bring out the pink flesh and rind striping.

directions: Give this ornamental Eureka sport full sun so its cream-and-green striped leaves and green-striped young fruit show best. Prune lightly to shape and remove any all-green reverted shoots that outcompete the variegation. Harvest when the stripes fade to solid yellow and the interior reveals its distinctive pink-tinted flesh.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm site with good drainage.

directions: This dense, well-foliaged Eureka-type makes a good dooryard tree for humid, warm regions. Keep it evenly watered and feed regularly to support its steady fruit load. Pick the smooth, seedy yellow lemons across a long season, with the main crop in winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a leading Spanish winter lemon for Mediterranean climates.

directions: Plant this vigorous, thorny Spanish lemon in full sun with excellent drainage. It fruits early and heavily, so support young branches and feed through spring and summer. Harvest the thin-skinned, juicy lemons from late autumn through winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Spanish variety valued for its late spring-summer crop.

directions: Site this Spanish lemon in full sun and give it steady summer water in dry climates. Its main harvest comes late in the season, extending fresh supply after other lemons finish. Pick the large, low-seed, thick-rinded fruit when fully colored.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a Lisbon-type popular in Australia and New Zealand.

directions: This compact Lisbon selection carries fruit inside the canopy, giving it some protection from sunburn and wind. Keep it well fed and evenly watered for smooth, thin-skinned fruit. Harvest the nearly seedless, fine-textured lemons mainly through winter and early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a frost-free, sheltered location.

directions: Plant this near-thornless, seedless lime in full sun with fast-draining soil and protect it from frost. Water consistently, as drought stress causes fruit drop, and feed with a citrus formula during active growth. Pick the fruit dark green for maximum acidity, before it yellows and softens.

planting.note: Plant in spring; commonly kept in containers for the aromatic leaves.

directions: Prized for its double-lobed, intensely fragrant leaves as much as its knobby fruit, this thorny tree grows well in a pot. Harvest leaves as needed year-round, cutting from the outer canopy to encourage flushes of fresh growth. Use the bumpy green fruit for its aromatic zest rather than its scant, sharp juice.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, frost-free position.

directions: Unlike tart limes, this variety yields mild, low-acid fruit with a distinctive sweet flavor. Plant in full sun with good drainage and water steadily during fruit development. Harvest when the thin rind turns pale greenish-yellow and the flesh is juicy and gently sweet.

planting.note: Plant in spring; extremely frost-sensitive and often container-grown.

directions: Grow this ceremonial citron as a small, thorny shrub or in a large pot that can shelter from any cold. Protect the fragile fruit from wind and scarring, and prune to keep an open, airy plant. Harvest the fragrant, thick-rinded, mostly-rind fruit while it holds a clean yellow color.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an Italian citron grown for candied peel.

directions: This classic Italian citron produces large, oblong fruit with a thick, fragrant rind used for candying. Grow it as a protected shrub or container plant and shield it from cold and wind. Harvest when the smooth-to-ribbed rind ripens to a bright yellow.

planting.note: Plant in spring; among the most cold-hardy of edible citrus.

directions: This tidy, slow-growing tree suits small gardens and containers and shrugs off light frost better than most citrus. Give it full sun and even moisture, and it needs little pruning beyond shaping. Eat the small oval fruit whole, skin and all, when it turns bright orange, enjoying the sweet rind against tart flesh.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a hardy, round-fruited kumquat.

directions: This thorny, bushy kumquat makes an attractive, cold-tolerant ornamental that also fruits well. Grow in full sun with regular water and prune only to maintain shape. Pick the small round orange fruit when fully colored and eat whole for its sweet skin and tangy pulp.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a popular ornamental houseplant citrus in cool climates.

directions: This compact kumquat-mandarin hybrid flowers and fruits almost continuously and grows happily in a bright pot indoors. Use a free-draining citrus mix, water when the top of the soil dries, and feed lightly through the growing season. Harvest the small, thin-skinned orange fruit anytime for its very sour juice, ideal for cooking and drinks.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, partly shaded spot.

directions: A named Australian selection with pink-blushed new growth and pale rose-colored pulp. Grow it like the wild finger lime, giving light shade in hot climates and steady moisture with good drainage. Harvest the elongated fruit when the skin darkens and softens slightly, revealing its blush-pink, effervescent vesicles.

planting.note: Plant in spring; shelter from wind and hard frost.

directions: This cultivar bears dark, blackish-green fruit with striking pink interior pearls. Site it in a protected position with dappled light in intense sun and keep the roots evenly moist. Pick the finger-shaped fruit once it softens, and squeeze out the pink vesicles to garnish dishes and drinks.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered position.

directions: An Australian selection known for reddish fruit and crimson-pink pulp. Grow in full to part sun with wind shelter, and protect from frost while young. Harvest when the fruit colors deeply and gives to gentle pressure, then scoop out the rosy, glistening juice pearls.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a finger-lime hybrid needing frost protection.

directions: This finger-lime hybrid produces small, egg-shaped fruit with deep red skin and blood-red flesh. Give it full sun in cooler areas, sharp drainage, and shelter from frost and drying wind. Harvest when the fruit turns burgundy and slightly soft, using the tart, crimson pulp as a striking garnish.

planting.note: Plant in spring; hardier than true limes thanks to its kumquat parent.

directions: This lime-kumquat cross makes a compact, cold-tolerant plant well suited to pots and small spaces. Grow in full sun with regular water and feed during the growing season for steady production. Harvest the small, pale-yellow fruit for its lime-like juice, and note the rind is mild enough to use as well.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an ornamental, cold-tolerant limequat.

directions: Slightly more upright and ornamental than Eustis, this limequat carries plentiful small fruit on a tidy plant. Give it full sun, even moisture, and a container it can overwinter indoors in cold regions. Pick the oval yellow fruit for tart, lime-flavored juice, using the whole fruit skin included.

planting.note: Set out grafted trees after the last hard frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Plant a grafted tree with the graft union well above the soil and mulch the root zone without touching the trunk. Water deeply but let the top few inches dry between soakings, and feed with a citrus fertilizer in spring and summer. Fruit ripens over winter and holds well on the tree; pick when the blush skin yields slightly and the flesh tastes sweet.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into a warm, sheltered, frost-free spot.

directions: Dig a hole no deeper than the root ball and firm the soil so the crown sits slightly high. Give this deeply red-fleshed cultivar full sun and steady summer moisture for the best color and sugar. Harvest through the cool months once fruit reaches full size and the rind loses its green cast.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this cultivar is especially cold-sensitive when young.

directions: Choose a wind-protected site and plant so the graft stays above grade. This deeply pigmented, nearly seedless grapefruit is sensitive to heat stress and cold, so keep it evenly watered and shelter it during frosts. Pick fruit in late winter when the ruby flesh is fully sweet and the skin is smooth.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a hot, frost-free location.

directions: Plant the grafted tree at its nursery depth in free-draining soil and water it in well. This classic white-fleshed, nearly seedless grapefruit needs long summer heat to build sugar. Leave fruit on the tree into winter and pick as the pale rind turns fully yellow.

planting.note: Transplant in spring once nights stay reliably above freezing.

directions: Give this vigorous, old-line seedy cultivar plenty of room, as it grows large and cold-hardy for a grapefruit. Keep the root zone mulched and water deeply during dry spells. The richly flavored fruit ripens in winter; harvest when full and heavy despite the many seeds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with good air drainage.

directions: Set the grafted tree high in well-drained ground and water consistently to develop the deep red interior. Feed three times through the growing season with a balanced citrus food. Pick the smooth, blushed fruit in winter when the flesh has colored and sweetened.

planting.note: Transplant in warming spring soil, sheltered from frost.

directions: Plant so the crown sits at or slightly above grade and keep the trunk base dry. This early pink-fleshed sport of Marsh needs sustained heat for sweetness. Harvest through winter as the nearly seedless fruit takes on its blush and softens slightly.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates cooler climates than true grapefruit.

directions: This pomelo-grapefruit cross ripens sweet without the heat that grapefruit demands, so it suits milder citrus zones. Plant in full sun with the graft above soil and water evenly. Pick the thick-rinded green-to-yellow fruit in late fall to winter once the sweet, low-acid flesh develops.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: A sister of Oroblanco, this pomelo-grapefruit hybrid bears very large, sweet, low-acid fruit on a vigorous tree. Give it full sun, deep watering, and steady feeding. Harvest the big yellow globes from late fall through winter when heavy and mild.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny, frost-protected spot.

directions: This low-acid mandarin and pomelo cross yields juicy, seedy, yellow-skinned fruit despite its grapefruit-like name. Plant in full sun and keep evenly moist through the fruiting season. Pick in winter when the very juicy fruit is soft and sweet; use promptly since it does not store long.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into deep, well-drained soil with full sun.

directions: This popular pink-fleshed pomelo produces very large, sweet fruit under a thick rind and pith. Plant the grafted tree high, water deeply, and protect it from frost while young. Harvest the heavy fruit in winter once the rind lightens and the segments taste mild and sweet.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a hot, sunny, frost-free position.

directions: A yellow-fleshed pomelo bearing large, firm, sweet fruit on a spreading tree. Give it full sun, room to grow, and deep periodic watering. Pick in winter when the fruit is full-sized and the thick rind turns pale yellow.

planting.note: Transplant in spring in full sun with frost protection.

directions: This pomelo hybrid ripens with a red star pattern in the flesh and a sweet berry-like flavor. Plant in full sun, keep steadily watered, and shelter from cold. Harvest in late winter to early spring when the interior develops its rosy blush.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, humid, frost-free climate.

directions: This natural tangelo bears loose, rough, greenish-orange rinds over remarkably sweet, easy-peeling flesh. Grow in full sun with steady warmth and moisture. Harvest in winter when the wrinkled skin puffs away from the fruit and the segments taste sweet.

planting.note: Transplant in spring in a warm, sunny, frost-free spot.

directions: This Puerto Rican orange-and-grapefruit hybrid peels like an orange but eats like a mild, sweet grapefruit. Plant in full sun with even moisture and frost shelter. Pick from late fall through winter once the large fruit colors and the rind loosens.

planting.note: Transplant in spring; hardier than most sour citrus.

directions: A small, tart Japanese citrus used green for its fragrant juice and zest. Plant in full sun with good drainage and give some cold protection in marginal areas. Pick the golf-ball-sized fruit while still green in early autumn, before it yellows, for the sharpest flavor.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with winter shelter in cool zones.

directions: This sour Japanese citrus is prized green for its juice much like sudachi but with larger fruit. Grow in full sun with free-draining soil and steady moisture. Harvest while still green and firm in early to mid autumn for the brightest, most aromatic juice.

planting.note: Set dormant vines in early spring once the ground can be worked.

directions: Plant the rootstock so the graft union sits well above the soil and tie the strongest cane to a sturdy trellis wire. Prune hard each winter to two or three fruiting spurs per position, keeping the canopy open for airflow. This late-ripening black wine grape colors deeply and reaches full sugar in mid to late autumn.

planting.note: Plant dormant grafted vines in spring after hard frosts pass.

directions: Train a single trunk up to the first trellis wire and remove suckers below the graft through the season. Thin excess shoots in early summer so clusters ripen evenly. This mid-season black grape softens early and is harvested a week or two ahead of Cabernet Sauvignon.

planting.note: Set out dormant vines in early spring in a cool-climate site.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and shelter the vine from extreme afternoon heat, which this thin-skinned grape resents. Prune to a modest crop load so the tight clusters ripen fully. Pick early in the harvest season, as the delicate black berries are prone to bunch rot if left too long.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring; guard early buds against late frost.

directions: Establish a permanent cordon along the wire and spur-prune each winter for a balanced crop. Because it buds early, choose a site with good air drainage to reduce spring frost damage. This white wine grape ripens in mid-season to golden-green clusters with high sugar.

planting.note: Set dormant grafted vines out in early spring.

directions: This vigorous vine needs firm summer pruning to keep the canopy open and light reaching the fruit. Position shoots on the trellis to expose clusters and preserve the grape's bright aromatics. Harvest the pale green berries in mid-season while acidity is still crisp.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring; this variety takes cold well.

directions: One of the more cold-tolerant vinifera whites, it still ripens slowly and rewards a long, cool autumn. Keep the crop moderate and expose the fruit zone with careful leaf removal. Let the golden clusters hang into late season to develop full aroma before picking.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring in a warm, sunny site.

directions: This vigorous red thrives in heat and needs a strong trellis to carry its long, drooping canes. Prune to spurs and support the fruiting wire, as heavy clusters can pull shoots down. Pick the small dark berries in mid to late season once sugars are high.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring where summers are warm and dry.

directions: Head-train or cordon-train this heat-loving vine and thin shoots to avoid overcrowding the compact clusters. Because berries within a bunch ripen unevenly, watch the fruit closely near harvest. Pick in mid to late season when most berries reach deep color and high sugar.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring in a hot, sunny location.

directions: This drought-tolerant red does well head-trained and handles wind and heat with ease. Keep a moderate crop so the late-ripening berries can build full sugar. Harvest the sweet dark fruit near the end of the season for the richest flavor.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring in a long-season, warm site.

directions: This vigorous Italian red buds early and ripens late, so give it a long, warm growing season. Spur-prune and manage vigor to keep the large clusters from overcropping. Pick the thin-skinned berries in late season once color and sugar peak.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring in a warm, sunny vineyard.

directions: This Spanish red ripens comparatively early, its name hinting at its quick maturing habit. Train to a cordon and spur-prune each winter for consistent crops. Harvest the thick-skinned dark berries in the earlier part of the red-grape season.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring in a long-season, sunny site.

directions: This demanding Italian red buds early yet ripens very late, needing warm autumns to finish. Give it your best sun-exposed slope and prune for an open, well-lit canopy. Harvest the pale-bloomed dark berries at the very end of the season when tannins mature.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring in a warm, sunny location.

directions: Plant this inky red where summers are warm and springs are frost-free, as it buds fairly early. Manage the canopy to expose clusters and reduce the risk of shatter at bloom. Pick the small dark berries in mid to late season for deep color and sugar.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring; tolerates cooler sites well.

directions: More cold- and disease-tolerant than its Cabernet relative, this red suits cooler regions and ripens a bit earlier. Train to a cordon and thin shoots for good light in the fruit zone. Harvest the medium dark berries in mid-season once flavors develop.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring in a hot, long-season site.

directions: This deeply colored blending red ripens very late and demands a warm autumn to finish. Give it full sun and a strong trellis, and spur-prune for a balanced crop. Harvest the small dark berries near the tail end of the season for full maturity.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring in a cool-climate location.

directions: This aromatic white prefers cooler regions where its pinkish berries hold acidity and perfume. Keep the crop moderate and protect the fruit from excessive heat. Harvest in mid-season once the distinctive spicy aroma develops in the clusters.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring in a warm, sunny site.

directions: This vigorous white keeps high acidity even as sugars climb, making it versatile in the vineyard. Manage vigor with summer pruning and expose clusters for even ripening. Harvest the golden berries from mid to late season depending on the style desired.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring in a warm-climate vineyard.

directions: This golden-skinned white is fairly easy to grow and yields reliably on a cordon system. Its thin skin makes it prone to noble rot in damp autumns, which some growers welcome for sweet wines. Harvest in mid-season, earlier for dry styles and later where botrytis is desired.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring in a warm, sunny location.

directions: This aromatic white ripens best in warmth and can be shy-bearing, so choose a sheltered, sunny spot. Keep the canopy open and let sugars build fully to bring out its floral character. Harvest the small golden berries in mid to late season when fully ripe.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring in a hot, sun-drenched site.

directions: This heat-demanding red ripens very late and needs long, warm autumns to develop fully. Head-train the drought-tolerant vine and keep a moderate crop load. Harvest the dark berries at the very end of the season once tannins and sugar peak.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring in a warm, sunny vineyard.

directions: This productive Italian red holds bright acidity and crops heavily, so thin clusters to keep quality up. Train to a cordon and spur-prune for consistent yields. Harvest the deeply colored berries in mid to late season once sugars are ample.

common_name: Muscat Blanc a Petits Grains Grape
scientific_name: Vitis vinifera 'Muscat Blanc a Petits Grains'

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring in a warm, sunny location.

directions: This intensely aromatic white produces small, sweet berries prized for both wine and the table. Keep the canopy open to protect the thin skins and preserve the perfumed aroma. Harvest in mid-season when the fragrance is strong and sugars are high.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring; suits cooler climates.

directions: A color mutation of Pinot Noir, this white bears grayish-pink berries in tight clusters. Grow it in a cool region and manage the crop for balance. Harvest the fruit in mid-season, picking earlier for crisp styles.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring in a cool, well-drained site.

directions: This Austrian white is vigorous and productive, so manage vigor and crop load carefully. It ripens in mid to late season and holds good acidity. Harvest the greenish-yellow clusters once the peppery character develops.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring; tolerates humid, coastal sites.

directions: This Iberian white handles humidity and coastal air better than most vinifera thanks to its thick skins. Keep the canopy open for airflow to limit disease. Harvest the small aromatic berries in mid-season with lively acidity intact.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring in a warm, sunny location.

directions: This Mediterranean white loves heat and tolerates dry conditions once established. Train to a cordon and spur-prune for balanced crops. Harvest the golden-green berries in mid to late season with good acidity and aroma.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring in a warm, sunny site.

directions: This early-ripening Italian red suits sites where the season is a little shorter. Train to a cordon and spur-prune, keeping the crop moderate. Harvest the deeply colored berries earlier than most reds, before autumn cools.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring in a warm, long-season site.

directions: This late-ripening red needs a long, warm autumn to lose its green edge and reach full flavor. Give it full sun and manage the canopy to expose fruit. Harvest the dark berries at the end of the season once they are fully mature.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring in a hot, sunny location.

directions: This vigorous seedless table grape needs cane pruning rather than spurs to fruit well. Provide a strong trellis and thin clusters for larger berries. Harvest the sweet pale-green fruit in late summer once fully colored and sugary.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring in a hot, sunny site.

directions: This early red seedless grape is crisp and sweet, ripening ahead of many table varieties. Give it full sun and thin the clusters for better size. Harvest the bright red berries in mid to late summer when firm and sweet.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring in a hot, long-season location.

directions: This late-ripening red seedless grape stores and ships well thanks to its firm, crunchy berries. It needs a warm autumn to develop full crimson color. Harvest in late season once the elongated berries turn deep red and sweeten.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring in a hot, sunny vineyard.

directions: This large-berried seeded table grape produces big, round pink-red fruit on strong clusters. Give it a robust trellis and thin bunches to size the berries. Harvest in late season when the berries reach full size and color.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring in a hot, sunny location.

directions: This heat-loving Muscat serves for table use, raisins, and sweet wines with its large fragrant berries. Give it full sun and a long, warm season to ripen fully. Harvest the amber-green fruit in late season when the aroma is intense.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring in a hot, sunny site.

directions: This large black seedless grape bears oval berries on generous clusters late in the season. Thin bunches and give it strong support for the heavy crop. Harvest in autumn once the berries turn glossy black and sweeten fully.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring; very cold-hardy and adaptable.

directions: This hardy American grape is easy to grow on a two-wire trellis and tolerates cold winters. Prune hard each winter, leaving a few renewal canes for next year's fruit. Harvest the slip-skin blue-black berries in early fall when deeply colored and fragrant.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring; hardy and vigorous.

directions: Often called the white Concord, this vigorous American grape is grown for fresh eating and juice. Train it on a trellis and prune to renewal canes each winter. Harvest the amber-green slip-skin berries in early fall when sweet and aromatic.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring; hardy American variety.

directions: This old American grape yields coppery-red berries used for juice, wine, and fresh eating. It ripens late, so give it a full, warm autumn. Harvest once the berries deepen to purplish-red and carry a musky aroma.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring; cold-hardy American type.

directions: This American grape bears small, sweet pink berries in compact clusters and ripens early. Its moderate vigor suits smaller trellises and home gardens. Harvest the tender-skinned fruit in late summer when soft and sugary.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring; hardy, early Concord type.

directions: This early blue-black American grape resembles Concord but ripens sooner, useful in short-season areas. Train on a trellis and prune to renewal canes each winter. Harvest the slip-skin berries in late summer to early fall when deeply colored.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring; notably cold-hardy seedless.

directions: This hardy seedless table grape bears sweet pink-red berries and withstands cold winters well. Train on a trellis and prune to renewal canes for reliable crops. Harvest the tender berries in late summer when they soften and sweeten.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring; disease-resistant and hardy.

directions: This vigorous, disease-resistant seedless grape bears blue-black slip-skin berries with a Concord-like flavor. It handles humidity well and needs little spraying. Harvest the clusters in late summer once fully blue and sweet.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring; hardy seedless white.

directions: This popular white seedless grape yields large loose clusters of sweet golden berries. It needs cane pruning and a strong trellis to carry its crop. Harvest in late summer when the berries turn amber and sugary.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring; cold-hardy seedless red.

directions: This hardy red seedless grape produces tight clusters of small, sweet berries with a spicy flavor. It crops heavily, so thin bunches to maintain size and quality. Harvest in late summer when the berries turn bright red and soften.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring; hardy French-American hybrid.

directions: This cold-tolerant white hybrid ripens late and its thick skins resist cracking, making it ideal for ice wine. Train on a trellis and prune for balanced crops. Harvest late, sometimes after a hard freeze when sweet wines are the goal.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring; hardy hybrid for cool regions.

directions: This productive white hybrid suits cooler climates and ripens in mid-season with good acidity. It crops heavily, so thin clusters to prevent overbearing and vine stress. Harvest the compact clusters once sugars reach the target for the intended wine style.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring; disease-resistant red hybrid.

directions: This disease-resistant red hybrid tolerates humid climates and yields deeply pigmented juice. It buds late, which helps it avoid spring frost damage. Harvest the dark clusters in mid to late season once fully colored.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring; very hardy early red hybrid.

directions: This very cold-hardy red hybrid ripens early, making it useful in short, cool seasons. Its small berries color quickly, so watch for birds as harvest nears. Pick the dark clusters in late summer to early fall once sugars are high.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring; hardy aromatic white hybrid.

directions: This hardy white hybrid carries the spicy aroma of its Gewurztraminer parent with better cold tolerance. Train on a trellis and manage the canopy for good fruit exposure. Harvest the golden clusters in mid to late season once the perfume develops.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring; hardy, disease-resistant native.

directions: This vigorous American native resists disease and yields small, intensely colored berries for robust red wine. It ripens late and needs a long, warm season to finish. Harvest the dark clusters at the end of the season once sugars peak.

planting.note: Set dormant vines out in spring; hardy early red hybrid.

directions: This hardy, vigorous red hybrid ripens early and holds bright acidity in the fruit. Its heavy vigor calls for firm pruning and a strong trellis. Harvest the small dark berries in late summer to early fall once colored and sweet.

planting.note: Plant dormant vines in spring; heat- and humidity-loving Southern grape.

directions: This classic bronze muscadine thrives in the humid South and resists the diseases that trouble other grapes. Give it wide spacing and a sturdy overhead or wire trellis, and prune to spurs in late winter. Harvest the large thick-skinned berries in early fall as they turn amber and drop easily when ripe.

planting.note: Set out rooted vines after the last spring frost, since new growth is easily killed by cold.

directions: Train this vigorous female vine on a sturdy T-bar or pergola trellis and plant a compatible male within about thirty feet for pollination. Prune in late winter to fruiting canes and thin dense growth each summer to let light reach the fruit. The large fuzzy brown fruit ripens in mid to late autumn and finishes softening indoors after harvest.

planting.note: Plant one male near several females once spring frost danger has passed.

directions: Grow this non-fruiting male vine solely to pollinate late-blooming female fuzzy kiwis such as Hayward, spacing one male for up to eight females. Provide strong support and prune lightly in winter, leaving enough wood to carry abundant flowers. Watch for a heavy flush of creamy blossoms in late spring that overlaps the females' bloom.

planting.note: Establish vines in spring after frost, siting them where late-season fruit can ripen.

directions: These vigorous dioecious vines need both a male and a female plant to set the familiar brown fuzzy fruit. Give them deep, well-drained soil, ample summer water, and a robust overhead trellis to carry the weight of a mature crop. Seed-grown plants take many years to fruit and reveal their sex, so named grafted or cutting-grown vines are far more reliable.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in a sheltered, sunny spot with wind protection.

directions: This smooth-skinned relative of the fuzzy kiwi bears yellow-fleshed fruit and blooms earlier, so match it with an early male pollinator. It is slightly more cold-tender than fuzzy kiwi and appreciates a warm, protected wall or slope. Harvest the bronze fruit in autumn and allow it to soften off the vine for the sweetest flavor.
